prompt: I've got a discovery call tomorrow at 2pm ET with Natalie Reeves, VP of Supply Chain at Ironclad Distribution -- they're a mid-market 3PL based in Memphis, about 600 employees, doing ~$180M revenue. S...
assertions: The brief follows the Type 1 Pre-Discovery Call Brief structure exactly: header line with prospect name and company, QUICK CONTEXT section, YOUR GOAL FOR THIS CALL section, DISCOVERY QUESTIONS section, ANTICIPATED OBJECTIONS AND RESPONSES section, CALL SUCCESS CRITERIA section (with primary/secondary/minimum), COMPETITORS TO LISTEN FOR section, and THINGS NOT TO DO section

Discovery questions total 5 or fewer across all subsections (opening, depth, qualification), every single one is phrased as a question ending in a question mark -- none are statements or instructions to the rep

QUICK CONTEXT is concise enough for a 2-minute read (roughly 3-5 sentences), references Natalie's whitepaper download and the cold call intel about manual spreadsheet tracking and 2-3 hours lost per dispatcher, and mentions the lost retail contract as a relevant trigger event

YOUR GOAL FOR THIS CALL states a specific, singular call goal -- not a vague 'learn about their needs' or 'do a demo' but a concrete outcome like qualifying the pain around exception tracking, identifying who else is in the buying process, and confirming if there is budget or timeline pressure
ANTICIPATED OBJECTIONS AND RESPONSES includes at least 2 objections with exact words the prospect might say and responses that validate before reframing -- not defensive or argumentative responses

THINGS NOT TO DO section is present and contains 3-5 specific warnings relevant to this call (e.g., do not demo on a discovery call, do not trash Oracle WMS, do not assume the lost contract means urgency)
